title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices Rise Amidst Declining Trade Volumes and Anticipated Biofuel Constraints
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(02nd â€“ 08th September)
  • A total of 118,854 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 42.80% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a fall of 1.7% as 703,436 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price was $55.14/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, a rise of 2.99%from the past week; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 5.79% in prices.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price increased by 3.22% WoW to reach $57.13MT last week; on 10th Sep, it was $56.63/MT.
  • In the RFS market,D3 RIN prices remained stable WoW, while D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices saw a rise of 3.51% each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

This weekâ€™s highlights indicate a notable shift in the CA LCFS and RFS markets, with a 42.80% drop in Type 1 credit exchanges and a 1.7% decline in all non-zero trades. Despite this decrease in transaction volume, the average CA LCFS credit price for Type 1 transfers rose by 2.99%, reflecting a broader upward trend as the average LCFS spot credit price increased by 3.22% week-over-week. These trends suggest that anticipated constraints on credit generation capacity for certain biofuels could lead to tight market. As a result, credit banks may experience reduced stockpiles, potentially driving up credit prices on a weekly basis. Therefore, stakeholders should brace for further increases in credit prices as dynamics. cCarbon has conducted an analysis of the public comments received by the CARB. Two amendments that received the most comments during the 15- Day Public Consultation period following CARBâ€™s release of text to modify the stateâ€™s Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) program are the 9% step-down in carbon intensity starting in Q1 2025, with auto acceleration not triggered before May 15, 2027, and the limitation of credits from biomass-based diesel produced from virgin soybean and canola oil to 20% of annual biomass-based diesel reported on a company-wide basis. A notable portion of the feedback favored tightening the carbon intensity reduction targets, with many commenters advocating for more aggressive goals to accelerate decarbonization.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_587643" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 2.99% WoW rise from $53.54 to $55.14 last week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices rose by 5.79% compared to the previous week. A total of 118,854 credits were exchanged in Type 1 trades, marking 42.8% fall WoW. The transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades also saw a fall of 1.7% last week to reach 703,436 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_587644" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 3.22% WoW, reaching $57.13/MT. On 10th Sep, the average spot price was $56.63/MT, up from $48.17/MT, the lowest point in August. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $57.67/MT as of 10th Sep.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_587645" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 34 trades were reported in the market, with seven high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $69.50 for 5,000 credits of Type 2, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $43.50 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 10,000 credits. The largest volume transfer of 200,000 credits in Type 2, was undertaken for a price of $69.25/MT, and the smallest volume transfers of 954 credits, a Type 1 category, undertaken for a price of $48.00/MT.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_587646" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] As August ends, trading volumes have dropped significantly to 1.90 MMT, compared to 5.21 MMT in July. July saw a rebound in trading volumes to levels not seen since April, but August saw a rise in Type 1 prices, which had been relatively stable in June and July. This upward trend in prices has continued into the first week of September. The proposed increase in the CI reduction target to 9% for 2025, up from the original 5% annual target, might renew interest among stakeholders. However, the cap on renewable diesel and biodiesel produced from soybean and canola oil could raise concerns among some producers.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_587647" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 02nd â€“ 08th Sep, total VPT recorded a 15.26% rise (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices remained stable WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each rose by 3.51% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Biden-Harris Administration Announces $62 Million to Support Americaâ€™s Growing Hydrogen Industry. In support of the Biden-Harris Administrationâ€™s Investing in America agenda, the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) today announced nearly $62 million forÂ 20 projects across 15 statesÂ to accelerate the research, development, demonstration, and deployment of next-generation clean hydrogen technologies. These investments support several end-uses for clean hydrogen, and will become an important part of the DOE Hydrogen Programâ€™s broad portfolio of research, development, and demonstration activities.
  • Clean Fuels Urges Treasury to Immediately Issue Â§45Z Guidance and Safe Harbors. Clean Fuels Alliance America sent a letter to Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen, urging the agency to issue guidance, including safe harbors, to the Â§45Z Clean Fuels Production Credit by September 1. The new producer credit becomes available on January 1, 2025, as the existing blender credit expires. Clean Fuelsâ€™ letter emphasizes the difficulties that farmers, producers, and fuel marketers are facing in making the transition without guidance on the rules.
  • BETO Selects Six Projects for Phase II Small Business Research and Development Grants. The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) announced awards totaling $142 million for small businesses through DOEâ€™s Small Business Innovation Research (SBIR) and Small Business Technology Transfer (STTR) Phase II funding. Across DOE, 123 projects in 34 states received funding to pursue clean energy research and development projects. The average Phase II award is $1.1 million for a period of two years.
  • Plug Awarded $10 Million DOE Grant to Lead Development of Advanced Hydrogen Refueling Station in Washington State. Plug Power Inc. (NASDAQ: PLUG), a global leader in comprehensive hydrogen solutions for the green hydrogen economy, has been awarded $10 million by the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) to demonstrate next generation hydrogen refueling infrastructure for medium and heavy-duty vehicles at scale. Plugâ€™s project, HYPER-Fuel (â€œthe projectâ€), is a liquid-to-gaseous dispensing and cryogenic dispensing hydrogen refueling station architecture. This funding is part of the DOEâ€™s $62 million investment in 20 projects across 15 states aimed at accelerating the deployment of next-generation clean hydrogen technologies. These initiatives will advance key aspects of hydrogen fueling infrastructure, demonstrate hydrogen-powered container-handling equipment for port operations, and enhance processes critical to the efficient, timely, and equitable implementation of hydrogen technologies.
